items.q: Why are infrared cameras used to detect warm objects in the dark?
items.correct: Warm objects emit infrared radiation due to their temperature; the camera detects this emission.
items.misconception: Infrared cameras shine infrared light onto objects so they reflect back visibly.
items.random_wrong: Infrared cameras detect heat moving through the air around objects.
items.why: Students assume cameras always work by reflection (like visible-light cameras), missing that thermal cameras passively detect emitted IR.

items.q: What happens to the current as it goes around a series circuit?
items.correct: It stays the same at every point — current is conserved.
items.misconception: Current gets used up by each component as it flows around the circuit.
items.random_wrong: Current splits in half at the battery so each direction gets some.
items.why: Students confuse current (charge flow) with energy (which is transferred), thinking the bulb 'uses up' current rather than energy.

items.q: A book on a high shelf has lots of energy in its:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: gravitational store. “kinetic store” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Petrol stores energy in which form?
items.why: It's chemical. “thermal” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many joules of work are done when a 10 N force moves an object 3 m?
items.why: The real figure is 30 J. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
